claim: Glyph positional constraints (specific glyphs appearing predominantly word-initially vs word-finally) reflect a syllabic or consonant-vowel encoding structure rather than grammatical affixes, where initial glyphs encode consonant onsets and final glyphs encode vowel codas of a CV or CVC syllable scheme.
evidence_for: Word-initial glyphs ['o','c','q','s','d'] and word-final glyphs ['y','n','l','r','o'] are highly distinct sets with minimal overlap
Average word length of 5.03 glyphs is consistent with 1-2 syllable encodings in a CVC scheme (2-3 glyphs per syllable)
Syllabic scripts typically show strong positional glyph constraints, matching observed behavior
The frequent words 'daiin', 'aiin', 'chedy', 'shedy' show consistent internal glyph sequencing suggestive of syllable templates
evidence_against: Only 25 unique glyphs is low for a full syllabary (typical syllabaries have 40-100 symbols), though it could represent a partial or consonantal syllabary
Glyph entropy (3.8627) is consistent with alphabetic encoding; syllabic scripts typically show lower per-symbol entropy due to larger symbol sets
The positional constraints could equally reflect grammatical morphology (prefixes/suffixes) in an alphabetic cipher
test: Compute mutual information between glyph identity and word position (initial, medial, final) for each of the 25 glyphs. Compare the resulting positional specificity profile against simulated profiles from known syllabic scripts (e.g., Linear B transliterated to glyph frequency data) vs alphabetic scripts with strong morphology (medieval Latin).
test_metric: Per-glyph positional mutual information score; bimodality index of glyph position distributions
test_threshold: If >15 of 25 glyphs show positional mutual information > 0.3 bits and the bimodality of position distributions matches syllabic script profiles more than alphabetic profiles (measured by KL divergence to reference), syllabic encoding is supported

tests_run.test: compute mutual information between glyph identity and word position (initial, medial, final) for eac
tests_run.details: Positional distinctness: 0.25. First-only glyphs: {'q'}. Last-only glyphs: {'r', 'm', 'n'}. Universal glyphs: {'a', 'l', 'o', 'd'}. Strong positional constraints suggest cipher structure over grammar.

reasoning: A syllabic encoding hypothesis, if confirmed, would drastically constrain the decipherment approach and could explain why alphabetic substitution attempts have consistently failed
